name: Philippines

colors.symbolism: equality and fraternity (the white triangle of the Katipunan)
the sun of liberty and the three stars representing the country's main island groups

description: A field divided per pall (three regions meeting at a point on the horizontal meridian): a blue triangle above, a red triangle below, and a white equilateral triangle along the hoist whose side equals the flag's height. Centred in the white hoist triangle is a yellow eight-rayed sun, with a small yellow five-pointed star at each corner of the triangle.
history: Designed by Emilio Aguinaldo and first unfurled on 12 June 1898 at the proclamation of independence from Spain in Kawit, Cavite. The design was suspended during American colonial rule (1907–1919, ban; full restoration 1919) and adopted in its present form by the Republic of the Philippines on Independence Day, 4 July 1946 (modern law: Republic Act No. 8491, the Flag and Heraldic Code of the Philippines, 1998). Uniquely, the flag is displayed with the red stripe up in time of war and blue up in time of peace — the only national flag with this property.

features.symbolism: the field is divided per pall into three triangles — blue above and red below evoke the colours of the United States and of the earlier Spanish Cross of Burgundy, while the white hoist triangle was the emblem of the Katipunan, the revolutionary society that fought Spanish rule, standing for equality and fraternity. The flag is uniquely flown with the red stripe up in time of war and blue up in time of peace

independence and the dawn of freedom; the eight primary rays represent the first eight provinces (Manila, Cavite, Bulacan, Pampanga, Nueva Ecija, Bataan, Laguna, and Batangas) placed under martial law by Spain at the start of the Philippine Revolution in 1896

features.role: three stars at the points of the triangle
features.symbolism: the country's three main island groups — Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao
